mnemonics.title: Two's Complement Magic
mnemonics.content: Flip all bits (1's comp), then add 1 — that's how you make a number negative!

mnemonics.title: Hex Groups of Four
mnemonics.content: Binary to Hex: 'Group by four, from right to left, 8421 rules the show!'

mnemonics.title: Signed Number Range
mnemonics.content: For n bits: Signed range is -2^(n-1) to +2^(n-1)-1, Unsigned is 0 to 2^n -1

mnemonics.title: Overflow Detectives
mnemonics.content: Overflow occurs when: + + = -, or - + - = + (sign bits misbehave!)

mnemonics.title: BCD Addition Trick
mnemonics.content: If decimal sum >9, add 6 (0110) to correct — BCD needs fixing after addition!

acronyms.usage: Each decimal digit represented by 4 binary bits (e.g., 9 = 1001)

acronyms.expansion: American Standard Code for Information Interchange
acronyms.usage: 7-bit code for characters (0-127), extended ASCII uses 8 bits (0-255)
Preferred method for signed numbers in computers; simplifies addition/subtraction

acronyms.expansion: Institute of Electrical and Electronics Engineers 754
acronyms.usage: Standard for floating-point arithmetic; defines single (32-bit) and double (64-bit) precision
Rightmost bit in binary representation; carries least weight (2^0)
Leftmost bit; for signed numbers, indicates positive (0) or negative (1)

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a mirror at the end of the binary string; flip everything on one side, then add 1 — that's your negative!
Think of adding binary like stacking coins: 1+1 creates a 'carry' stack that moves to the next column (left)
Picture a slider: sign bit is direction, exponent is zoom level, mantissa is the actual number shape
A cup (fixed bits) can only hold so much — when it overflows, liquid (data) spills to the sign bit and ruins the result!
Each hex digit is a 4-bit 'word' — visualize them as 4-letter memory addresses mapping to binary patterns

analogies.analogy: Like a 24-hour clock: if it's 3 o'clock and you want -5 hours, go to 10 (3-5 mod 12 gives 10) — the wrap-around creates negatives!
Unsigned is like a car's odometer (0 to max), Signed is like a temperature gauge (can go below zero into negatives)
Like counting on fingers but forgetting modulo — after 9:59 comes 10:00 on a digital clock, losing the 'carry' info
Each bit is like a light switch: OFF=0, ON=1 — computers have billions of tiny switches flickering on/off
Like zoom levels on Google Maps — close zoom shows every street (precise), far zoom shows just countries (less precise but covers range)
Like flipping a photo to negative — every 0 becomes 1, every 1 becomes 0; useful but creates two zeros (+0 and -0)
